title: SERP Clickability Audit — Top 12 Zero-Click Queries (Day 4 Phase 13C.5)
methodology: Each page assessed across 8 dimensions: title-query match, meta persuasiveness, number/result inclusion, title genericness, meta genericness, wrong-page ranking, better target exists, page strength

audits.meta_answers_searcher: PARTIAL — says 'Calculate your monthly payment' but doesn't give the $405.53 upfront
audits.title_has_number: YES — $405.53 (but may be truncated in SERP)
audits.meta_too_generic: PARTIAL — could include the actual answer
audits.google_ranking_wrong_page: NO — blog post is correct target
audits.better_target_exists: NO — this is the best page
audits.page_strong_enough: YES — 2,400+ words, calculator embed, amortization table
audits.click_likelihood: MEDIUM — Title is strong but position 11 means below fold on most screens. 68-char truncation in SERP may cut $405.53.

audits.meta_answers_searcher: NO — says 'reveals critical insights' but doesn't say what those insights are
audits.title_has_number: NO — missing 2025 (the year is key for this query)
audits.meta_too_generic: YES — 'critical insights' is entirely generic. Replace with 1-2 specific Fed findings
audits.google_ranking_wrong_page: NO — this blog post is the right page
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Blog post is relevant but competing against .gov domain (federalreserve.gov) and major news sites
audits.click_likelihood: LOW — Meta doesn't give any reason to click. User will click Fed.gov or CNBC which have authority + specific data in snippet

audits.meta_answers_searcher: NO — doesn't give the actual rate number
audits.title_has_number: YES — '5 weeks' but missing the actual rate %
audits.meta_too_generic: YES — 'latest mortgage rate analysis' without the rate %
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Good analysis but competing with real-time rate data from Bankrate/NerdWallet/Freddie Mac
audits.click_likelihood: LOW — Users scanning SERP for 'mortgage rates' see competitor results with actual rate numbers in titles/snippets. QFINHUB's title lacks the specific percentage

audits.query: how much mortgage can I afford 100k salary
audits.title_matches_query: YES — 'How Much Mortgage Can I Afford on $100K Salary'
audits.meta_answers_searcher: YES — but this is a PROBLEM. Meta gives $320K-$420K range, which may satisfy user without clicking
audits.meta_too_generic: NO — actually too specific (snippet cannibalization risk)
audits.better_target_exists: PARTIAL — /calculators/mortgage-affordability is the tool but this blog post ranks better for the query
audits.page_strong_enough: YES — Strong content with exact numbers
audits.click_likelihood: LOW — Meta gives away the answer ($320K-$420K). User gets what they need from the SERP snippet. Fix: tease without revealing exact range

audits.query: how to pay off credit card debt fast
audits.title_matches_query: YES — exact match on 'how to pay off credit card debt fast'
audits.meta_answers_searcher: PARTIAL — mentions '7 proven strategies' but doesn't hint at which ones
audits.title_has_number: YES — '2026' and implied '7 strategies' in meta
audits.title_too_generic: NO — but 'Proven Strategies That Actually Work' is a common listicle pattern
audits.meta_too_generic: PARTIAL — could name at least 1-2 specific strategies
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Good content but competing with NerdWallet, Bankrate, Experian which dominate this query
audits.click_likelihood: MEDIUM — Title is solid but position 14 means competing against household name brands

audits.meta_answers_searcher: YES — mentions comparison vs Bankrate, NerdWallet, Zillow
audits.google_ranking_wrong_page: PARTIAL — comparison page ranks but users may want the actual calculator, not a comparison
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Good comparison but lacks interactive calculator element
audits.click_likelihood: MEDIUM — Comparison angle differentiates from pure calculator results, but some users want the tool directly

audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Timely content but competing with SSA.gov, AARP, major news
audits.click_likelihood: MEDIUM — Title is strong with '3.9%' number. Position 18 + competing against .gov/major news limits clicks

audits.meta_answers_searcher: PARTIAL — mentions 4% rule but doesn't give example numbers
audits.title_too_generic: PARTIAL — 'Plan Your Retirement Income' is generic tagline
audits.meta_too_generic: PARTIAL — could show a specific scenario result
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — Blog explains concept but searchers want the calculator
audits.click_likelihood: LOW — Position 22 + blog post about a calculator vs. actual calculator tools. User expects interactive calculator

audits.meta_answers_searcher: PARTIAL — mentions FIRE movement, savings rates
audits.title_has_number: NO — doesn't give the 'how much' answer in title
audits.title_too_generic: PARTIAL — 'How Much Do You Need Saved?' is the question, not the answer
audits.page_strong_enough: MEDIUM — FIRE niche competitive, dominated by Mr. Money Mustache, etc.
audits.click_likelihood: LOW — Position 25 + generic title + competitive niche

audits.title_matches_query: PARTIAL — includes parameters but title is keyword-stuffed
audits.meta_answers_searcher: PARTIAL — 'How much house can you afford' but robotic
audits.title_too_generic: NO — but title reads as robotic/SEO: '$100,000 Income $40,000 Down 6.5% Rate Mortgage Affordability Calculator 2026 — Free Online Tool'
audits.google_ranking_wrong_page: YES — /calculators/mortgage-affordability is the better, more flexible page for this query
audits.page_strong_enough: LOW — Overly specific tool variant with robotic title. Better funnel to main calculator
audits.click_likelihood: VERY LOW — Position 30 + robotic title. User has already seen 29 better results

key_findings.titles_truncated_in_serp: 2 of 12 — 20000-loan ($405.53 may get cut), mortgage-rates (long title)
key_findings.wrong_page_ranking: 4 of 12 — best-mortgage-calculator (compare vs tool), investment-calc-withdrawals (blog vs calculator), retire-by-40 (blog vs calculator), afford-100k (variant vs main calc)
key_findings.generic_title_or_meta: 5 of 12 — fed-report, mortgage-rates, credit-card-debt, investment-withdrawals, retire-by-40
